questions.question: What is delegation in the context of class design?
choices: Passing all method calls to a parent class via inheritance
Wrapping an embedded object and forwarding method calls to it
Creating multiple instances of the same class
Using global functions instead of methods
questions.explanation: Delegation is a design pattern where a wrapper class embeds another object and forwards (delegates) method calls to it. This is a form of composition and is often implemented using __getattr__ to intercept attribute lookups and redirect them.

questions.question: What is the key difference between composition and inheritance?
choices: Composition is faster; inheritance is slower
Inheritance models 'is-a' relationships; composition models 'has-a' relationships
Composition only works with built-in types
There is no difference; they are interchangeable
questions.explanation: Inheritance expresses an 'is-a' relationship (a Manager is a Person), while composition expresses a 'has-a' relationship (a Department has Employees). Composition embeds objects as attributes rather than inheriting from their classes.

questions.question: Python's pseudoprivate name mangling transforms an attribute named __spam inside class Ham into _________spam.
questions.explanation: Python name mangling transforms attributes that start with two underscores (and have at most one trailing underscore) by prefixing them with _ClassName. So __spam in class Ham becomes _Ham__spam. This helps avoid name clashes in class hierarchies.

questions.question: What is the purpose of pseudoprivate name mangling (double underscore prefix)?
choices: To make attributes truly private and inaccessible from outside the class
To prevent accidental name clashes in class hierarchies when subclasses define attributes with the same name
To improve performance by caching attribute lookups
To encrypt the attribute values
questions.explanation: Pseudoprivate names are not truly private -- they can still be accessed via the mangled name (_ClassName__X). Their purpose is to localize names to the class that created them, preventing accidental collisions when multiple classes in a hierarchy use the same attribute name.

questions.question: In Python 3, what is a bound method?
choices: A function defined outside of any class
A method that is associated with a specific instance and automatically receives that instance as self
A method that cannot be overridden by subclasses
A method that has been decorated with @staticmethod
questions.explanation: A bound method is a method object that pairs a function with a specific instance. When you access a method through an instance (obj.method), Python creates a bound method that automatically passes the instance as the first argument (self) when called.

questions.question: A class that defines method signatures but does not implement them, requiring subclasses to provide the implementation, is called an ________ superclass.
questions.explanation: An abstract superclass defines a common interface by declaring methods that subclasses must override. In Python, you can create abstract classes informally (by raising NotImplementedError) or formally using the abc module's ABC base class and @abstractmethod decorator.

questions.question: Which approach does Python use to formally enforce the interface of an abstract superclass?
choices: Compile-time type checking that prevents instantiation
The abc module with ABC and @abstractmethod, which raises TypeError if abstract methods are not overridden
A special 'interface' keyword
Abstract classes cannot be created in Python
questions.explanation: Python's abc module provides the ABC base class and @abstractmethod decorator. If a subclass does not override all abstract methods, attempting to instantiate it raises a TypeError. This is the formal mechanism for abstract classes in Python.
